paragraphs: The Australian round lime (Citrus australis), also known as the Gympie lime, dooja, native lime or round lime, is a citrus endemic to the lowland subtropical rainforests of south east Queensland, from around Brisbane northwards. In the wild it can reach about 20 metres, though it is far smaller in cultivation. The round to slightly pear shaped fruit is roughly 2.5 to 5 cm across with a notably thick skin.

The juice is sharply acidic and is best used for cordials, marmalades, sauces and flavouring rather than eaten fresh, while the thick skin lends itself to candied peel and zest. The species is of scientific interest too: University of Queensland researchers sequenced its genome because Citrus australis is recognised as resistant to Huanglongbing (citrus greening), a disease that devastates commercial citrus overseas.

claims.text: Citrus australis is endemic to the lowland subtropical rainforests of south east Queensland, from around Brisbane northwards.
It is also known as the Gympie lime, dooja, native lime and round lime.
In the wild it can reach about 20 metres, and it is far smaller in cultivation.
The fruit is round to slightly pear shaped, roughly 2.5 to 5 cm across, with a notably thick skin.
The acidic juice is best used for cordials, marmalades, sauces and flavouring rather than fresh eating.
University of Queensland researchers sequenced its genome because Citrus australis is recognised as resistant to Huanglongbing (citrus greening).

sources.name: University of Queensland News, Australian fruit holds the key to citrus disease resistance

paragraphs: The Indonesian lime sold by Australian nurseries is jeruk limo (also jeruk limau), the Java lime Citrus amblycarpa. PROSEA records it as known exclusively from cultivation in Java, Indonesia, where its fruit are a common market item. It is a hybrid lime, distinct from makrut (kaffir) lime, although its aromatic leaves are used as a substitute for makrut (Citrus hystrix) leaves to lift the taste of meat sauces.

The small depressed globose fruit (about 1.5 to 3.5 cm across) has a bullate, dimpled peel and yellow-green, sour, intensely fragrant pulp. The juice of immature fruit is the prized part, used as a condiment in Indonesian dishes such as sambal, soto and bakmi. The plant is a shrub or low tree to around 7 m, carrying white fragrant flowers.

claims.text: The Indonesian lime sold in Australia is jeruk limo (jeruk limau), Citrus amblycarpa.
Citrus amblycarpa is known exclusively from cultivation in Java, Indonesia.
Its aromatic leaves are used as a substitute for makrut (Citrus hystrix) leaves to flavour meat sauces.
The fruit is a small depressed globose berry about 1.5 to 3.5 cm across, with a bullate peel and yellow-green, sour, fragrant pulp.
The juice of immature fruit is used as a condiment in Indonesian dishes such as sambal, soto and bakmi.
The plant is a shrub or low tree up to about 7 m with white fragrant flowers.

sources.name: Ross Creek Tropicals: Indonesian Lime (Jeruk Limau)
The Thorny Fruit Co: Jeruk Limo, Indonesian Lime, Citrus Amblycarpa

paragraphs: Jeruk limo (jeruk limau) is the Indonesian lime Citrus amblycarpa, a hybrid lime that PROSEA records as known only from cultivation in Java, Indonesia. Despite sometimes being lumped in with makrut (kaffir) lime, it is a separate citrus, though its leaves serve the same role, used as a substitute for makrut (Citrus hystrix) leaves to improve the taste of meat sauces.

The fruit is a small depressed globose berry (about 1.5 to 3.5 cm across) with a bullate, dimpled peel and yellow-green, sour, very fragrant pulp. The juice of immature fruit is the part most valued, used as a condiment in sambal, soto and bakmi. The tree is a shrub or low tree to around 7 m, bearing white fragrant flowers.

claims.text: Jeruk limo (jeruk limau) is the Indonesian lime Citrus amblycarpa.
Citrus amblycarpa is known exclusively from cultivation in Java, Indonesia.
Its leaves are used as a substitute for makrut (Citrus hystrix) leaves to flavour meat sauces.
The fruit is a small depressed globose berry about 1.5 to 3.5 cm across with a bullate peel and yellow-green, sour, fragrant pulp.
The juice of immature fruit is used as a condiment in dishes such as sambal, soto and bakmi.
The plant is a shrub or low tree up to about 7 m with white fragrant flowers.

sources.name: The Thorny Fruit Co: Jeruk Limo, Indonesian Lime, Citrus Amblycarpa

paragraphs: The Kaffir lime (Citrus hystrix), increasingly called makrut lime (a more neutral name, as kaffir derives from an ethnic slur), is native to tropical Southeast Asia. It is instantly recognisable by its distinctive double, hourglass-shaped leaves, which are actually a leaf blade plus a winged, leaf-like petiole.

The small fruit has a bumpy, warty rind and yields little juice, so the plant is grown chiefly for its intensely aromatic leaves and zest, which are central to Thai and other Southeast Asian curries, pastes and soups.

claims.text: Citrus hystrix, increasingly called makrut lime (a more neutral name, as kaffir derives from an ethnic slur)
Native to tropical Southeast Asia
Recognisable by distinctive double, hourglass-shaped leaves (a leaf blade plus a winged, leaf-like petiole)
Small fruit with a bumpy, warty rind that yields little juice
Grown chiefly for its aromatic leaves and zest, central to Thai and Southeast Asian cooking

paragraphs: The Kusaie lime is a yellow fruited form of the Rangpur, an old citrus type that almost certainly originated in India. According to the UC Riverside Citrus Variety Collection it was introduced into Hawaii from Kusaie Island in the Caroline group in 1885 and carried to the United States in 1914. Genomic work shows the Rangpur is a hybrid of citron and mandarin, so despite the name lime the fruit sits botanically closer to a mandarin while keeping a sharp, lime like acidity.

Fruit colour runs from yellowish to reddish orange, with orange flesh that is tender, juicy and strongly acid. The seeds are small, numerous and highly polyembryonic, which lets the variety come remarkably true from seed. The collection notes that calling it a lime is misleading, since the fruit resembles a mandarin in form rather than a true lime.

claims.text: Kusaie is a yellow fruited form of the Rangpur that almost certainly originated in India, introduced to Hawaii from Kusaie Island in 1885 and to the United States in 1914.
The Rangpur is genomically a hybrid of citron (Citrus medica) and mandarin (Citrus reticulata).
The rind is yellowish to reddish orange with orange flesh that is tender, juicy and strongly acid.
Seeds are small, numerous and highly polyembryonic, so the variety reproduces remarkably true from seed.
The name lime is regarded as misleading because the fruit resembles a mandarin rather than a true lime.

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UC Riverside, Kusaie Rangpur lime (CRC 452)

paragraphs: Mosambi is the Indian sweet lime, Citrus limetta, also called sweet lemon. It is native to southern Iran and is also grown around the Mediterranean Basin. Despite the lime in its name, it is a sweet, low-acid citrus rather than a tart one, and the ripe fruit reads more like a small greenish-yellow orange than a true lime.

Its flavour is sweet and mild, with a marked lack of acidity, so it tastes flatter than most citrus. The fresh juice turns bitter within a few minutes of exposure to air, so it is drunk straight after juicing. In the Indian subcontinent it is the most common citrus juice, valued as a refreshing drink rather than a cooking acid.

claims.text: Mosambi is the Indian sweet lime (sweet lemon), Citrus limetta.
It is native to southern Iran and is also cultivated in the Mediterranean Basin.
Its flavour is sweet and mild with a lack of acidity, tasting flatter than most citrus.
The juice turns bitter within a few minutes of exposure to air.
It is the most common citrus juice available in the Indian subcontinent.
The ripe fruit is greenish-yellow and looks more like a small orange than a true lime.

paragraphs: The Mount White lime (Citrus garrawayi, sometimes placed in the genus Microcitrus) is one of the citrus species endemic to Australia. It is a rainforest understorey shrub or small tree of the Cape York region in far north Queensland. Because its range is small and isolated, the species is regarded as rare, and there has been no commercial use of it to date.

It is a finger lime relative, carrying elongated, finger shaped fruit that are yellowish green with green pulp, set off by broad lanceolate leaves. The species was first described by F.M. Bailey in 1904 and is recorded in Australian botanical references under the common name Mount White Lime.

claims.text: Citrus garrawayi (Mount White lime) is native to the Cape York region of far north Queensland, growing as a rainforest understorey shrub or small tree.
Its range is small and isolated, so the species is rare and has had no commercial use to date.
The fruit is elongated and finger shaped, yellowish green with green pulp, and the leaves are broad and lanceolate.
The species was first described by F.M. Bailey in 1904 and carries the common name Mount White Lime.

sources.name: Atlas of Living Australia, Citrus garrawayi (Mount White Lime)

paragraphs: The Rangpur lime (Citrus x limonia) is a lime in name only. Genomic work shows it is an F1 hybrid of a female citron (Citrus medica) and a male mandarin (Citrus reticulata), so it is really a mandarin-lime. Named for Rangpur in Bangladesh, it is also called the mandarin-lime in the United States and limao-cravo (cravo lemon) in Brazil. The fruit is unusual for a lime: it has an orange, yellowish to reddish-orange rind and orange flesh.

Despite the orange colour, the tender, juicy flesh is strongly acidic with a sour, lime-like flavour, which makes it useful in cooking and drinks. Its biggest role, however, is horticultural: it is a vigorous, hardy citrus rootstock tolerant of the tristeza virus and soil-borne disease, used heavily in Brazil. In cooler areas it is also grown as an ornamental.

claims.text: Rangpur lime (Citrus x limonia) is an F1 hybrid of a female citron and a male mandarin, also called mandarin-lime and limao-cravo.
The fruit has an orange to reddish-orange rind and orange flesh.
The flesh is tender, juicy, and strongly acidic with a sour, lime-like flavour.
It is widely used as a hardy citrus rootstock tolerant of tristeza virus, especially in Brazil.

sources.name: Rangpur lime, Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UC Riverside

paragraphs: The Red Centre Lime is a CSIRO-bred native citrus hybrid of the Australian finger lime (Citrus australasica) and an acid mandarin (described in some accounts as the Rangpur lime, Citrus x limonia). It is closely associated with, and often sold alongside, the Australian Blood Lime. The striking fruit has a deep red, glossy, slightly dimpled skin in a small oval to teardrop shape, with flesh edged or tinged in pink and red.

Like its finger lime parent it carries juice vesicles (caviar-like pearls), but the juice is also spread through the fruit, so it is easier to juice than a pure finger lime. The flavour combines the spicy zing of the finger lime with the aromatic acidity of the mandarin parent for a sweet-sour profile. It suits sauces, preserves, condiments, beverages, and fresh garnish, and was granted Plant Breeder's Rights in 2004.

claims.text: The Red Centre Lime is a CSIRO hybrid of the finger lime and an acid mandarin, associated with the Australian Blood Lime, with some sources naming the Rangpur lime (Citrus x limonia) as the second parent.
The fruit has a deep red, glossy, slightly dimpled skin in a small oval to teardrop shape, with pink to red tinged flesh.
It contains finger-lime-style juice pearls but juice is also spread through the fruit, making it easier to juice.
The flavour is a sweet-sour combination of finger lime zing and mandarin aromatics, used in sauces and preserves.

sources.name: A taste of the outback, CSIRO lime varieties, CSIROpedia
Native lime, Citrus hybrid Red Centre Lime, Gardening With Angus

paragraphs: The Russell River lime (Citrus inodora, also placed in Microcitrus) is an Australian native citrus endemic to the lowland tropical rainforests near the Bellenden Ker Range and Cape Tribulation in far north Queensland. It grows as a shrub up to about 4 metres and is now rare, listed as endangered under Queensland law after much of its habitat was cleared for banana and sugar cane.

Its standout quirk is written into the name: the species epithet inodora means without odour, and its leaves and flowers are essentially odourless, lacking the aromatic oils typical of citrus. The fruit is egg shaped and yellowish green. It is also known as the large leaf Australian wild lime.

claims.text: Citrus inodora is endemic to lowland tropical rainforest near the Bellenden Ker Range and Cape Tribulation in far north Queensland.
It grows as a shrub up to about 4 metres tall.
It is rare and listed as endangered under Queensland's Nature Conservation Act, with much habitat cleared for banana and sugar cane.
Its leaves and flowers are essentially odourless, lacking the aromatic oils characteristic of citrus, reflected in the epithet inodora.
The fruit is egg shaped and yellowish green, and the plant is also called the large leaf Australian wild lime.

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UC Riverside, Microcitrus inodora (CRC 3785)

paragraphs: Sublime is an Australian dwarf selection of the Tahitian lime (Citrus latifolia), sold for compact gardens, patios and pots. It grows on its own root system rather than being grafted, and it stays naturally small, reaching about 1.5 metres in the ground and staying under a metre high with a similar width in a container, which suits courtyards and even balconies while still producing full-sized juicy limes.

claims.text: Sublime is an Australian marketed dwarf selection of the Tahitian lime (Citrus latifolia).
It grows on its own root system rather than being grafted, and produces large, juicy limes.
It reaches about 1.5 metres in the ground and stays under a metre high with a similar width in a pot, suiting patios and balconies.

sources.name: The Garden Gurus, Citrus Lemonicious and Sublime

paragraphs: Sudachi (Citrus sudachi) is a small Japanese acid citrus, the specialty and symbol of Tokushima Prefecture, which grows about 98 per cent of Japan's crop. Genetic analysis confirms it is a hybrid rather than a true species, with one parent being yuzu and the other an unidentified relative of Japanese citrus such as koji and tachibana. The fruit is round and small (roughly 20 to 40 grams) and turns yellow-orange when mature.

It is almost always harvested and used while still green, when its sharp, clean acidity and intense aroma are most pronounced (often likened to lime with herbal, peppery notes). The tart juice and zest replace lemon or lime in sauces, marinades, dressings, and drinks, and halved sudachi is a traditional garnish for grilled fish, soba, and udon. It is not eaten as a table fruit.

claims.text: Sudachi (Citrus sudachi) is a hybrid, with yuzu as one parent and an unidentified Japanese citrus relative as the other.
It is the specialty of Tokushima Prefecture, which produces about 98 per cent of Japan's sudachi.
The fruit is small and round and is harvested and used while still green.
It has sharp acidity and intense aroma, and its juice and zest replace lemon or lime in sauces, marinades, and as a garnish for fish, soba, and udon.

sources.name: Sudachi ichandrin (papeda hybrid), Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UC Riverside

paragraphs: The Sunrise Lime (often Australian Sunrise Lime) is a CSIRO-bred native citrus hybrid, a cross between the Australian finger lime (Citrus australasica) and a calamondin (itself a mandarin and cumquat cross). It was selected from a faustrimedin, with the parent tree chosen in 1990 at CSIRO. The result is a distinctive small fruit shaped like a pear in golden orange hues, appearing in winter.

Unlike its finger lime parent, the Sunrise can be eaten whole because the rind and pith are sweet, much like a kumquat, while the juice inside is tangy and fresh with a delicate floral note. It works fresh as a garnish or in sauces, marmalade, beverages, and preserves. It is one of three CSIRO native lime varieties (with Australian Blood and Australian Outback) granted Plant Breeder's Rights in 2004.

claims.text: The Sunrise Lime is a CSIRO-bred cross between the finger lime (Citrus australasica) and a calamondin, selected from a faustrimedin.
The parent tree was selected in 1990 at CSIRO.
The fruit is pear-shaped and golden/orange, appearing in winter.
The fruit can be eaten whole because the rind and pith are sweet, with tangy juice and a floral note.
It is one of three CSIRO native lime varieties used in sauces and marmalade.

sources.name: A taste of the outback, CSIRO lime varieties, CSIROpedia
Citrus Sunrise Lime, Gardening With Angus

paragraphs: The sweet lime, best known as the Palestine sweet lime (Citrus limettioides, sometimes placed under Citrus limetta), is an oddity among limes: a low-acid citrus that lacks the sharp bite of ordinary limes and lemons. The small, round to slightly oblong fruits have a thin, smooth rind with prominent oil glands that ripens from pale green to orange-yellow, and a pale yellow, very juicy, tender flesh.

Its defining trait is the near absence of acidity. Reference descriptions call the flavour insipid or bland because of the lack of acid, sometimes with a slightly bitter aftertaste, which makes it appealing to people who want citrus flavour without tartness. Long grown across India, the Near East, Egypt, and the Mediterranean as a fresh juice fruit, it is also used as a citrus rootstock.

claims.text: The Palestine sweet lime is a low-acid lime, scientific name Citrus limettioides (also referred to as Citrus limetta).
Fruits are small and round to oblong with a thin smooth rind ripening pale green to orange-yellow, and pale yellow very juicy flesh.
Its flavour is insipid or bland due to a lack of acid, sometimes with a slightly bitter aftertaste.
It is grown in India, the Near East, Egypt, and the Mediterranean as a fresh juice fruit and is also used as a rootstock.

sources.name: Palestine sweet lime, Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UC Riverside

paragraphs: The Tahitian lime is the Persian lime (Citrus latifolia), also sold as Bearss or seedless lime. It is a triploid hybrid, generally regarded as a cross of Key lime with lemon; the Bearss selection arose around 1895 from a seedling of Tahitian origin at Porterville, California.

Being triploid, the tree is essentially sterile, so the fruit is reliably seedless. It is larger and thicker-skinned than the Key lime, with juicy, acidic flesh that lacks the Key lime's bitterness, and it is the most widely grown lime worldwide.

claims.text: Is the Persian lime (Citrus latifolia), also known as Bearss or seedless lime
A triploid hybrid generally regarded as a cross of Key lime with lemon
The Bearss selection arose around 1895 from a seedling of Tahitian origin at Porterville, California
Being triploid the tree is essentially sterile, so the fruit is reliably seedless
Larger and thicker-skinned than the Key lime, with juicy acidic flesh that lacks the Key lime's bitterness
The most widely grown lime worldwide

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Bearss lime

paragraphs: The West Indian lime (Citrus aurantiifolia), also called the Key, Mexican, or true lime, is the small, intensely aromatic lime behind Key lime pie. Wikipedia describes it as a natural hybrid of Citrus micrantha (a wild papeda) and citron (Citrus medica), and it is widely treated as its own species. Fruits are small (roughly 3 to 6 cm across), almost round, and ripen from green to yellow with an exceptionally thin, fragrant rind.

Compared with the larger seedless Persian (Tahitian) lime, it is smaller, seedier, more acidic, and more aromatic. The greenish-yellow flesh is very juicy, highly acidic, and prized for its complex sour fragrance, which is why it is favoured for drinks, dressings, marinades, and ceviche. The trees are cold sensitive and the seeds are strongly polyembryonic, so they often grow true to type.

claims.text: It is a natural hybrid of Citrus micrantha (a wild papeda) and citron, treated botanically as a species.
Also known as Key lime, Mexican lime, and West Indian lime.
Fruits are small, almost round, with a thin fragrant rind, ripening green to yellow.
It is smaller, seedier, more acidic, and more aromatic than the Persian lime.
Flesh is greenish-yellow, very juicy, and highly acidic; seeds are strongly polyembryonic.
It is the flavouring ingredient in Key lime pie.

sources.name: Mexican lime, Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UC Riverside
